architecture.summary: Large Concept Models (LCM) operate on sentence-level embeddings from the SONAR space rather than tokens. The input is segmented into sentences, each encoded to a fixed-size vector using frozen SONAR encoders. A transformer-based model (decoder-only) then processes sequences of these concept embeddings autoregressively to predict the next concept. The generated concept embeddings are decoded back into text (or speech) using frozen SONAR decoders, enabling language- and modality-agnostic reasoning. Multiple architectures are explored: Base-LCM (MSE regression), One-Tower Diffusion LCM (single transformer predicting clean embeddings from noisy input), Two-Tower Diffusion LCM (separate contextualizer and denoiser with cross-attention), and Quantized LCM (residual vector quantization of SONAR space followed by discrete or continuous prediction).
trained_components: Input normalizer (robust scaler fit to SONAR vectors)
Two-Tower diffusion LCM contextualizer and denoiser
SONAR decoder fine-tuned for quantized representations

biblio.title: Large Concept Models: Language Modeling in a Sentence Representation Space

claims.statement: Models trained using 1.6B parameter models and training data in the order of 1.3T tokens
One architecture scaled to a model size of 7B parameters and training data of about 2.7T tokens

baseline.name: existing LLMs of the same size

claims.statement: LCM exhibits impressive zero-shot generalization performance to many languages, outperforming existing LLMs of the same size
SONAR embedding space supports 200 languages for text input and output, 76 languages for speech input, and speech output in English
SaT Capped sentence segmentation method with 200 character capping achieves higher AutoBLEU scores than SpaCy Capped across all sentence lengths
Residual Vector Quantization with all 64 codebooks reaches around 70% of the auto-encoding BLEU score achieved with continuous SONAR embeddings
LCM operates on sequences which are at least an order of magnitude shorter than token sequences, assuming an average sentence length of 10–20 tokens
The model can be applied to any language and modality supported by SONAR encoders in zero-shot without additional data or fine-tuning
